CG-Probes — Recovering clinically-grounded directions in patient-query embeddings

Self-contained results report. Generated from the benchmark cache. Lead metric is quadratic-weighted κ on the clinician gold (§3); the silver benchmark and separability use macro-F1. Silver vs golden is explained in §2.

Summary. We test whether three clinical safety axes — Medical Urgency (MU), Psychological Urgency (PU), and Topic Sensitivity (ET, the intrinsic emotional load of the subject), each graded 0/1/2 — can be read off a frozen text embedder as single linear directions (a dot product), with no model fine-tuning. They can. On held-out, cluster-disjoint data the probes separate all three axes well above chance, and against the two-oncologist clinician gold a frozen-encoder linear probe is level with the deployable safety LLM (gpt-oss-safeguard-20b) on the urgency axes (MU, PU) and trails the larger reasoning LLMs only modestly; the topic-load axis (ET) is the one place the LLMs' world knowledge wins. The probe is a single forward pass through a frozen encoder — no token generation — so at the system level it is ~10²–10³× cheaper per query than an autoregressive safety LLM, and its cost is flat per added policy axis (all axes read one shared embedding), so a 10–15-signal policy is essentially free. (If the query embedding is already computed — e.g. reused from the RAG retriever — the marginal probe cost is one dot product, ~0.3 µs.)

1. Pipeline & models

The full pipeline and the model used at each step. The operating language is Czech: the generative and annotation steps use models picked for Czech fluency, and all four frozen embedders officially support Czech (multilingual).

stepmodel(s)role
1. Query clusteringQwen3-Embedding-4B → UMAP → HDBSCAN; cluster cards by gpt-5.4-minigroup 79,658 de-duplicated real queries into ~1,889 topic superclusters; defines the cluster-disjoint train/test split
2. Contrastive pair synthesisgemini-3.5-flash (generate) + gpt-5.4-mini (blind verify)create the safety-critical grades (MU2, PU1/PU2) that are near-absent in real logs; the verifier is a different model family, so a positive can’t be a shared-generator artifact
3. Golden reference annotationgpt-5.4 (virtual annotator)an independent third grade alongside the two oncologists on the 200-item gold (itself excluded from the adjudicated gold)
4. Frozen embedders under testQwen3-Embedding-8B, gemini-embedding-2, text-embedding-3-large, Harrier-27bthe frozen spaces in which we probe for linear safety directions — no fine-tuning
5. Deployable safety-LLM baselinesgpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-safeguard-20bthe one-call policy classifiers the probe is benchmarked against
Model selection. Czech is lower-resource, so model choice matters. Across candidates we eyeballed Czech output quality, and GPT (gpt-5.4 family) was clearly the strongest at Czech — especially at imitating the patient register and real search-query style (short, lay, diacritic-free, often misspelled). It is therefore used for synthesis verification and as the virtual annotator. The embedders were chosen for Czech / multilingual coverage; Harrier-27b — English-first in origin but officially Czech-supporting and near the top of the MTEB leaderboard — is included as a strong general-purpose contrast, so the linear-recoverability result is not tied to one embedder family. (It still underperforms the multilingual Qwen/gemini on these Czech clinical-safety axes — §3.1 — so a top general embedder is not automatically best here.) Probe directions and thresholds are fit only on the cluster-disjoint contrastive split — never on the clinician gold; the headline then reports, per axis, the strongest embedder cell (which cell is shown in §3.1).

The three safety axes (clinician-defined, each graded 0/1/2): MU — medical / somatic urgency (0 = benign, 2 = somatic emergency needing EMS); PU — psychological urgency (0 = no distress, 2 = suicidal intent); ET — Topic Sensitivity, the intrinsic emotional load of the topic (0 = benign, 2 = prognosis / end-of-life / body-integrity), graded independent of tone. Source: 79,658 de-duplicated Czech oncology search queries clustered into ~1,889 superclusters (the cluster-disjoint train/test split).

The probe. For each axis we take a single difference-in-means direction on L2-normalized embeddings (mean of grade-2 minus topic-matched grade-0), project a query onto it, and split the 1-D projection into 0/1/2 with two tuned thresholds — scoring a query is one dot product. The rare safety-critical grades absent in real logs (MU2, PU1/PU2) are synthesized and cross-model verified (§1, step 2); directions and thresholds are fit only on the cluster-disjoint contrastive split, never on the clinician gold.

2. How to read the numbers

Two label sets — silver and golden.
  • Silver = labels from the pipeline (cluster picker + a gpt-5.4 reference grade), on ~400 queries. Cheap and immediate — so the early experiments (separability, the embedder × instruction ablation, the geometry and validity checks) were run on silver without waiting for the clinicians, because it was already good enough to establish the method.
  • Golden = the clinician ground truth: 200 queries each graded by two oncologists with adjudication (§3). This is the benchmark that counts; the headline (§3) is scored against it.
Why both sets. Silver enabled fast iteration at larger N; golden then confirms the conclusions hold against real clinicians. The silver→golden delta (§3.3) shows the headline barely moves (MU/PU <0.04 QWK) — i.e. silver was a faithful stand-in, not a shortcut that flattered the result.
  • QWK (quadratic-weighted κ) is the lead metric on the clinician gold (§3): ordinal agreement that credits near-misses and penalizes 0↔2 confusions, and the same metric the oncologists' inter-rater agreement is reported in. 0 = chance, 1 = perfect.
  • macro-F1 averages the F1 of each of the 3 grades equally, so a model can't win by only predicting the common grade (≈0.33 = chance). Used for separability and the other silver-based checks.
  • Separability = macro-F1 on the held-out, cluster-disjoint contrastive TEST split — “is the axis linearly present at all?”, no human labels involved.
  • Numbers are reported real-only (synthetic excluded) as the headline, plus all, because synthetic rare-grade items can flatter the score.

3. Headline — clinician gold (N≈200, two oncologists)

Clinician-gold benchmark. Every system is scored against the clinician gold — 200 queries each labelled by two oncologists (package “Golden v1 — Core”), gold = adjudicated value or the agreed value; the LLM judge is excluded from gold. Lead metric = quadratic-weighted κ (ordinal agreement); the dashed line is the inter-oncologist agreement ceiling — what two humans achieve. Numbers are real-only (synthetic excluded). The method sections (§4–§7) use the larger silver (pipeline-label) set, run before the clinicians; the silver→human delta is in §3.3.

3.1 Per-axis κ vs clinician gold (real-only)

axishuman ceiling κDoM probesafeguard-20bgpt-oss-120bgpt-5.4 (frontier)n
MU0.7810.6520.6390.7180.72989
PU0.9580.8060.7610.8910.97989
ET0.819 (raw 0.319)†0.4860.8260.6660.84188
Reading. On the urgency axes MU and PU the frozen-encoder DoM probe shows no detectable difference from the deployable LLMs, though the eval is underpowered to prove equivalence. Paired on the same 89 real items, ΔQWK (probe−LLM) is small with every 95% CI including 0 — vs safeguard·low +0.02 (MU) / +0.05 (PU); vs gpt-oss-120b −0.06 / −0.08. A TOST equivalence test (α=0.05) does not clear a tight ±0.10 margin (90% CIs reach ±0.15–0.18), so at n=89 we certify equivalence only within ≈±0.18. Point estimates: MU probe 0.65 vs safeguard 0.64 / 120b 0.72; PU 0.81 vs 0.76 / 0.89. Only the frontier gpt-5.4 pulls clear on PU (0.98 [0.93,1.00], which exceeds the 0.96 ceiling = sampling variance). So the claim is competitive with the deployable LLMs (no significant difference) at far lower marginal cost, not proven equivalence or a point-estimate win. On ET the probe is the weakest system; we treat ET as a probe-side negative result (topic-confounded, η²=0.81 — §7.2) and make no ET system ranking (wide CIs at n≈89; the reconciled gold encodes one coding convention, so a high ET score partly reflects sharing it). The headline probe is the interpretable 1-D difference-in-means direction; the full-linear head (used to improve ET) is in §3.4. †The ET ceiling (0.82) is the reliability of the reconciled codebook (raw inter-rater κ 0.32; one under-specified cell, systematically adjudicated — §3.2; raw shown as the faint tick in F2h).
Full-set vs real-only ceiling. The dashed line in F2h and the “human ceiling κ” column above are the full-set inter-oncologist QWK (all 200 items, synthetic included). On the real-only queries the systems are actually scored on, inter-oncologist agreement is markedly lower — MU 0.49, PU 0.91, ET 0.32 (§3.5) — because real grade-2 cases are rare and genuinely ambiguous for humans too. So on real queries the probe matches or exceeds the real-only human ceiling on MU (0.65 vs 0.49) and sits just under it on PU; we headline the conservative full-set ceiling and report the real-only ceiling in §3.5, so the human bar is neither over- nor under-stated.

3.2 Why ET inter-rater agreement is low — a single codebook ambiguity

ET inter-oncologist agreement is low (κ=0.32, Krippendorff α=0.19), but the disagreement is not random: it is near-deterministic and concentrated on one boundary.

  • 100/101 disagreements go one direction (one rater grades higher).
  • That over-rating concentrates on the ET1→ET2 cell (n=98), where 0.85 of items are somatic-urgent (MU≥1) vs only 0.20 elsewhere — a 4× enrichment.
  • One rater coded acute somatic urgency as high emotional load (ET2); the codebook intended ET2 for long-term emotional/existential load, with somatic urgency staying ET1.
  • Reconciling this one ambiguity lifts ET agreement to κ=0.82 — on par with MU/PU.

Interpretation: ET’s raw α understates reliability; it reflects a single codebook underspecification, not construct unreliability. The adjudicated gold applies the codebook-intended rule (ET2 = long-term emotional load, not somatic severity), so the ET benchmark is on the corrected boundary.

3.3 Does the silver-label benchmark hold up against clinicians?

axiscellsilver κhuman κΔ
MUgemini/per_axis0.6700.652-0.018
PUqwen8b/generic0.7710.806+0.035
ETgemini/generic0.6400.486-0.154

MU/PU move ≤0.04 κ from silver to clinician gold — the pre-clinician conclusions hold. The 1-D DoM ET drops ~0.15 (silver over-credited it), consistent with ET being the contested axis — and the reason ET uses the full-linear head (§3.4), which is silver-stable. This is why silver was a usable stand-in: every method experiment (separability, instruction conditioning, geometry, validity) was run on silver before the clinician set existed, and the one comparison re-checkable against clinicians — the probe vs the deployable LLMs — keeps its ranking (probe level with safeguard on MU/PU, behind on ET).

3.4 The probe head: one direction (interpretable) vs a linear subspace

The headline probe (§3.1) is a single difference-in-means direction per axis — the geometric claim — which is near-optimal for MU/PU but underfits ET. For ET we additionally fit a class-balanced full-linear head in the same frozen space. Below we compare 1-D DoM, the full-linear head, and a non-linear MLP, all selected on the cluster-disjoint contrastive TEST and evaluated on clinician gold. (We also tried ordinal, whitening, and 4-embedder concatenation heads — all underperformed the full-linear head; benchmark/probe_extra.py.)

axis1-D DoM (κ)full-linear (κ)MLP (κ)ceiling
MU0.6520.6760.6000.781
PU0.8060.8170.7880.958
ET0.4860.5800.5800.819
Result. The full-linear head lifts ET 0.49→0.58 κ (macro-F1 0.66→0.74) — and the MLP does no better, so the gain is from capacity, not non-linearity: ET is a linear subspace, not a single direction. Same conclusion as the orthogonality strip (F5), the linear-vs-MLP ablation (F8) and the counterfactual test — ET is topic-distributed. We therefore keep the 1-D DoM direction as the headline (a single clinician-meaningful vector, and tied with any heavier head on MU/PU — it even beats full-linear on the contrastive TEST), and use the full-linear head only for ET, where one direction underfits. To push ET past ~0.58 the lever is training data, not the head — MU-decorrelated ET pairs — since ordinal/whitening/concat heads did not help.

4. Does each axis separate at all? (no human labels)

Best separability per axis on the held-out contrastive test split:

  • MU: 0.746 (gemini/generic) — clear separation, well above the 0.33 chance line.
  • PU: 0.961 (gemini/per_axis) — strong separation, well above the 0.33 chance line.
  • ET: 0.702 (gemini/generic) — clear separation, well above the 0.33 chance line.

All three axes are linearly present. PU separates most strongly (but it is the most synthetic-aided axis); ET separates on 100% real data, so it is the most trustworthy single result.

This is the central claim made directly visible: each axis's held-out queries, projected onto the single probe direction, sort into ordered grade bands (0<1<2).

5. Embedders & the instruction-conditioning ablation

Frozen embedders can be conditioned with a task instruction prepended to the query. We tried three modes — none (raw query), generic (one shared instruction), per_axis (an axis-specific instruction) — and scored the probe on the silver set (this ablation predates the clinician gold; see §2). The exact instructions used:

modeinstruction (prepended to the Czech query)
none— raw query, no instruction
generic (all axes)Represent the given Czech oncology patient query for classification along clinical-safety dimensions: medical urgency, psychological urgency, and emotional load.
per_axis · MUClassify the medical urgency of the given Czech oncology patient query: the somatic emergency level, from no urgency to requiring immediate emergency medical services, ignoring psychological aspects.
per_axis · PUClassify the psychological urgency of the given Czech oncology patient query: the level of emotional distress, existential concern, or suicidal intent expressed, ignoring the somatic medical content.
per_axis · ETClassify the intrinsic emotional load of the given Czech oncology patient query's topic, independent of the tone in which it is phrased.

Instructions are identical across the instruction-following embedders (Qwen/gemini/Harrier); OpenAI text-embedding-3 takes no instruction, so its generic falls back to raw. A positive-only per_axis_pos variant (dropping the “ignoring …” clause) was also tested — it did not rescue ET (note below).

Silver real-only macro-F1 for every embedder × instruction mode:

axis MU PU ET
embedder mode
gemini generic 0.581 0.695 0.729
none 0.320 0.543 0.669
per_axis 0.666 0.665 0.404
per_axis_pos 0.665 0.672 0.310
harrier27b generic 0.573 0.525 0.538
none 0.420 0.568 0.625
per_axis 0.611 0.628 0.485
per_axis_pos 0.625 0.598 0.503
openai3large generic 0.375 0.627 0.609
qwen8b generic 0.458 0.644 0.496
none 0.378 0.556 0.594
per_axis 0.538 0.716 0.526
per_axis_pos 0.445 0.716 0.488
Finding. Conditioning the embedder on a per-axis instruction is not uniformly good. It helps the intent axes (MU, PU) but hurts the topic axis (ET) on every embedder — most dramatically gemini ET (0.73 generic → 0.40 per-axis). We tested whether negation in the instruction was to blame (a positive-only variant): it was not — removing negation did not rescue ET. The mechanism: ET signal is the topic, so any axis-focusing instruction pulls the embedding away from the topical content the ET direction rides on. Best practice: per-axis instruction for MU/PU, generic/raw for ET.

6. Are the three axes geometrically distinct?

Cosine between the learned axis directions (1.0 = identical direction, 0 = orthogonal). Low off-diagonal = the axes are not redundant.

gemini / generic
MUPUET
MU+1.00+0.49+0.19
PU+0.49+1.00+0.19
ET+0.19+0.19+1.00
gemini / none
MUPUET
MU+1.00+0.73+0.10
PU+0.73+1.00+0.13
ET+0.10+0.13+1.00
gemini / per_axis
MUPUET
MU+1.00+0.59+0.44
PU+0.59+1.00+0.50
ET+0.44+0.50+1.00

MU↔PU correlate moderately (~0.4–0.6, both are “urgency”); ET is the most independent (cosine ~0.1–0.5 with the others). The axes are distinct directions, not three views of one signal — which justifies treating them separately. Per-axis instruction tends to increase these cosines (it entangles the axes), another reason it can hurt.

7. Are the probes valid? (extra tests, no clinician labels)

Separability and golden macro-F1 alone don't rule out three failure modes a reviewer will raise: the probe could be a topic detector, the signal could be a non-linear artifact, or macro-F1 could hide ordinal errors. We ran five checks that need no human labels.

7.1 Ordinal & safety-critical metrics (golden, real-only)

macro-F1 treats a 0→2 error like a 0→1 error. QWK (quadratic-weighted κ) penalizes far-off ordinal errors; Spearman ρ is the threshold-free monotonic association between the raw projection and the grade; AUROC≥2 is the deployable “flag the emergency” operating characteristic; F1(g2) is the safety-critical class alone.

AxiscellQWKSpearman ρAUROC ≥1AUROC ≥2F1(g0)F1(g1)F1(g2)n
MUgemini/per_axis0.6700.7040.8760.9580.7730.6020.623290
PUqwen8b/generic0.7710.7560.9660.9320.9390.7920.200290
ETgemini/generic0.6400.5890.9950.7560.9110.7980.477290
Reading. AUROC≥2 (rank-quality of the emergency flag) is the strongest column — the raw projection ranks the safety-critical cases well even where the thresholded F1(g2) is modest, i.e. the signal is there and only the operating point needs tuning. The thin grade-1 band (few real items) is where most F1 is lost.

7.2 Topic-orthogonality — is it just a topic detector?

For each axis direction we measured the cosine to every BERTopic supercluster's grade-0 centroid (a pure-topic anchor, built from benign queries so no grade signal leaks in). Low cosine ⇒ the axis is not aligned with topic.

Axiscellmean |cos|p95 |cos|max |cos|η² (proj~topic)verdict
MUgemini/per_axis0.250.430.500.12near_orthogonal
PUqwen8b/generic0.350.530.750.22near_orthogonal
ETgemini/generic0.210.470.560.81topic_confounded

The decisive metric is η² (variance of the projection explained by topic-cluster membership), not mean cosine: a probe can have low cosine to any single centroid yet still be predicted by which cluster a query is in. MU (η²≈0.12) and PU (η²≈0.22) are not topic detectors — low cosine and low η² agree. ET (η²≈0.81) is topic-confounded: topic membership explains ~81% of where an ET query lands, despite a low mean cosine — so the 1-D ET probe is substantially a topic detector, consistent with every other ET result. The probes encode safety, not topic, for MU/PU; ET does not.

7.3 Is a linear probe enough?

If a non-linear read-out of the same embedding did much better, “linearly recoverable” would be overstated. We compare the 1-D difference-in-means direction, a full-dimensional linear (logistic) probe, and a 1-hidden-layer MLP — all on the held-out test split.

Finding. The MLP barely beats the full linear probe anywhere (non-linearity buys ≈0) — so “linearly recoverable” is earned. The 1-D direction is near-optimal for MU/PU, but for ET it loses ~0.17 to the full-linear probe: ET is genuinely multi-directional (a topic family, not one axis) — converging with 7.2 and the instruction ablation.

7.4 Selectivity — concept, or exploitable structure?

Hewitt & Liang's control task: refit the same probe on grade-shuffled labels (identical marginals, no real signal). A high real−control gap means the probe reads the concept rather than incidental embedding structure.

7.5 Counterfactual minimal pairs — does it move for the right reason?

For grade-1 seeds we generated, holding topic constant, a paraphrase (grade unchanged — projection should not move) and minimal flip-up / flip-down edits (grade ±1 — projection should move, in the right direction). Effects are Δprojection in units of the axis's projection SD.

embedderaxisinvariance (Δ paraphrase)sensitivity (Δ flip)ratiodirection-correct
geminiMU0.471.773.740.94
geminiPU0.301.906.240.99
geminiET0.470.901.920.78
openai3largeMU0.971.601.640.88
openai3largePU0.591.161.960.86
openai3largeET0.740.741.000.57
Finding. For MU and PU a grade-flip moves the projection several× more than a paraphrase, in the correct direction ~90–99% of the time — strong behavioural (quasi-causal) evidence the probe tracks the safety concept itself. For ET the ratio collapses toward 1 and direction-correctness toward chance: a minimal ET grade-flip necessarily changes the topic, so a 1-D probe can't tell it from a paraphrase — the same ET-is-multidimensional story again.

7.6 Confusion & the PU–ET entanglement, visualized

8. Inference cost & deployability on one GPU

A probe scores a query with one forward pass through the frozen encoder (no token generation) followed by a dot product; all three axes read the one embedding. At the system level that decode-free encoder pass is ~10²–10³× cheaper than the autoregressive LLM, which decodes hundreds–thousands of tokens per query. Where the embedding is already computed (e.g. reused from the RAG retriever's index) the marginal probe cost is just the dot product, ~0.3 µs (the ~10⁶–10⁷× figures below). The deployable LLM baseline (gpt-oss-safeguard-20b) is one triage call per query; three per-axis calls would triple GPU occupancy, which a single dedicated hospital GPU cannot absorb. All LLM latencies below are measured on the serving GPU (Nvidia L40S 48 GB, HF Inference Endpoint, vLLM).

systemreasoningsingle-query latency (drained cluster)vs probe (marginal)
linear probe (marginal, embedding precomputed)—~0.3 µs (one dot product)1×
safeguard-20b (deployed)lowmean 2.88 s · median 2.60 · p95 4.52~10×10⁶
safeguard-20bmediummean 6.96 s · p95 11.27—
safeguard-20bhigherratic ~13–60 s (up to ~10k reasoning tokens; one cold start hit 941 s)—

Throughput ceiling: 4×L40S replicas sustained ~2.1 queries/sec at medium reasoning (concurrency 32; a single replica ~0.5–1.5 q/s). Latency is generated-tokens ÷ GPU decode-rate — the L40S decodes this 20B model at ~100–150 tok/s single-stream, so a faster GPU (H100 ≈ 3–4× bandwidth) shrinks the constant but not the gap (~10²–10³× vs a decode-free encoder pass; ~10⁶–10⁷× marginal once the embedding is computed). The embedding is computed once and shared across all three axes.

8.1 Reasoning effort: more is slower and less safe

Counter-intuitively, raising the safety model's reasoning effort lowers its MU accuracy and emergency recall while multiplying latency: with more reasoning it talks itself into reading a bare clinical term (“prasklé střevo” = ruptured bowel) as an educational lookup → MU0. We deploy low reasoning — fastest and best.

reasoningMU F1/QWKPU F1/QWKET F1/QWKemergencies missedlatency
low0.61/0.640.76/0.760.88/0.838/282.88 s
medium0.52/0.550.80/0.750.89/0.8412/286.96 s
Accuracy columns are real-only vs the oncologist gold; emergencies are the silver set's 28 clear MU2 cases. Even at its best (low), safeguard-20b grades 8 of 28 clear emergencies as benign (hemoptysis, airway edema, acute limb ischemia, overdose) — an irreducible floor from over-literal policy-following on bare Czech terms plus a 20B model's weaker Czech. For the same 28 pipeline-labeled emergencies the headline 1-D DoM MU probe grades 0/28 as benign at its deployed threshold (the rest downgraded to MU1 = “see a physician,” not dismissed) — the head-to-head safety comparison favours the probe. A prior confound is now fixed: the client had silently dropped reasoning_effort for gpt-oss models (the routing regex matched only gpt-5/o-series), so the earlier baseline ran at the vLLM default (~medium); reasoning is now passed via extra_body and swept.

9. Caveats

  • Clinician gold is in hand (headline = §3). The benchmark is now against a 200-item two-oncologist gold with a reported IAA ceiling; the silver (pipeline + gpt-5.4) 400-item numbers in §4–§7 are retained only as a larger-N robustness view. MU/PU shift <0.04 QWK between silver and clinician gold (§3.3).
  • ET gold boundary is partly definitional. The ET1/ET2 inter-rater split is low (raw κ 0.32) because of a documented codebook ambiguity (somatic urgency miscoded as high emotional load); the adjudicated gold and the reported ET ceiling apply the codebook-reconciled rule (κ 0.82), with the raw value always disclosed (§3.2).
  • Synthetic safety cells. MU2/PU2 training positives are 100% synthetic; mitigated by the generator-mismatch design + verifier gate + reporting real-only, but disclose. The 200-item clinician set is 90 real + 110 synthetic, also reported ±synthetic.
  • Skewed marginals & small grade-2 cells on a single Czech oncology setting — real MU2/PU2 are thin, so we lead with QWK/AUROC≥2 over per-grade-2 F1; generalization untested.
  • PU separability is inflated by synthetic positives (0.9+ on the contrastive set); the clinician real-only QWK (~0.81) is the operative number.

Cells: 39 (4 embedders × instruction modes × 3 axes). Baselines: gpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-safeguard-20b. Best-probe selection = max real-only golden macro-F1 per axis.
